items.q: What does an INNER JOIN return?
items.correct: Only rows with matching values in both tables.
items.misconception: All rows from both tables combined.
items.random_wrong: Only the inner columns of each table.
items.why: Students confuse INNER (intersection) with FULL OUTER (union). INNER drops unmatched rows from BOTH sides; LEFT keeps all from left; RIGHT keeps all from right; FULL OUTER keeps everything.

items.q: What is abstraction in computer science?
items.correct: Removing unnecessary detail to focus on essential features of a problem.
items.misconception: Making something abstract art or vague.
items.random_wrong: Writing code that does not work.
items.why: Students borrow the everyday meaning of 'vague'. In CS, abstraction is a precise technique: ignoring details that don't matter for the current task, while keeping what does.

items.q: What is the difference between OR and XOR?
items.correct: OR is true when at least one input is true; XOR is true when exactly one input is true.
items.misconception: They are the same gate with different names.
items.random_wrong: OR is for numbers, XOR is for letters.
items.why: Students miss the critical case: when BOTH inputs are 1. OR gives 1, XOR gives 0. XOR is 'exclusive' - excludes the both-true case.

items.q: Which is a high-level programming language?
items.why: The answer examiners want is: Python. “Assembly” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.
Correct answer: Integrated Development Environment. “Internet Data Engine” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: In Python, how many spaces are conventionally used for one level of indentation (PEP 8)?
items.why: Exact answer: 4 spaces. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
